<style>
  :root{

  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}

  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);

  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}

    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;

  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;

  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}

  aside.related .ttl{

Morse theory

Pick a smooth function $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$ whose critical points are non-degenerate, watch the sublevel sets $\{f\le c\}$ grow as $c$ increases, and read off the topology of $M$ from the indices of the critical points. The local picture (Hessian) controls the global picture (handles, cells, homology) — that is the whole idea.

blocks.html:
1. Morse functions

To extract the topology of $M$ from a function $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$, we need $f$ to be generic enough that its critical behaviour is non-degenerate everywhere. The Morse condition is the right genericity requirement — strong enough to force a quadratic-form normal form at every critical point, weak enough to be satisfied by a dense set of smooth functions.

Let $M$ be a smooth $n$-manifold and $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$ smooth. A point $p\in M$ is a critical point of $f$ if $df_p=0$. At a critical point the Hessian $$H_p f \colon T_pM\times T_pM \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$$ is a well-defined symmetric bilinear form (the choice of chart drops out because $df_p=0$). The critical point is non-degenerate if $H_pf$ is non-degenerate as a bilinear form, equivalently the Hessian matrix in any chart is invertible.

A function is a Morse function if all of its critical points are non-degenerate. The Morse index $\ind(p)$ is the number of negative eigenvalues of $H_pf$ — the dimension of the maximal subspace on which the Hessian is negative-definite.

Morse lemma. Around any non-degenerate critical point $p$ of index $k$, there is a chart $(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ with $p\mapsto 0$ in which $$f = f(p) - x_1^2 - \cdots - x_k^2 + x_{k+1}^2 + \cdots + x_n^2.$$ So locally, every Morse critical point looks the same — only the index distinguishes them.

Widget · height function on the torusdrag the slider; critical points light up
0°

A vertically-mounted torus has four critical points of the height function $h(x,y,z)=z$: two minima and maxima at the top/bottom rims (indices $0,2$) and two saddles where the inner equator meets the level set (indices $1,1$). Tilting away from vertical perturbs the critical-point positions but keeps the count: Morse functions form an open set.

Why insist on non-degeneracy? Two reasons.

  1. Isolation. The Morse lemma quadratic form has $0$ as its only critical point, so each non-degenerate critical point is isolated. Functions like $x^4$ on $\mathbb{R}$ have a degenerate critical point at $0$ that is non-isolated under perturbation.
  2. Density. By Sard's theorem and a perturbation argument, Morse functions are open and dense in $C^\infty(M,\mathbb{R})$: every smooth $f$ can be approximated arbitrarily well by a Morse function. So nothing is lost by working only with Morse functions.

See also
  • Smooth manifolds and atlases
  • Tangent spaces

Used in
Morse theory · Handle decomposition via sublevel sets
</section>

2. Handle decomposition

Fix a Morse function $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$ on a closed manifold and consider the family of sublevel sets $$M^c = \{p\in M : f(p)\le c\}.$$ As $c$ increases from $\min f$ to $\max f$, $M^c$ grows from empty to all of $M$. Two fundamental theorems describe how.

Theorem A (regular intervals). If $[a,b]$ contains no critical values of $f$, then $M^a$ is a deformation retract of $M^b$, and $M^b\setminus \mathrm{int}(M^a)$ is diffeomorphic to a collar $\partial M^a\times [a,b]$. (Proof: integrate the rescaled negative gradient $-\nabla f / \|\nabla f\|^2$.)
Theorem B (crossing a critical value). If $[c-\epsilon,c+\epsilon]$ contains a single critical value with one critical point $p$ of index $k$, then up to diffeomorphism $$M^{c+\epsilon} \;\cong\; M^{c-\epsilon} \cup_\varphi (D^k\times D^{n-k}),$$ where the $k$-handle $D^k\times D^{n-k}$ is glued along $\partial D^k\times D^{n-k}\subset \partial M^{c-\epsilon}$.

So topology is built handle-by-handle. The cell that "matters" — the descending disk — has dimension equal to the Morse index $k$; the transverse $D^{n-k}$ is just thickening to keep things a manifold.

Widget · sublevel sets of a height functiondrag $c$ from min to max; watch handles glue on
$c=-1.0$

Schematic of the four sublevel-set transitions for an upright torus, $f=z$: empty $\to$ disk (0-handle, min) $\to$ cylinder (1-handle, saddle) $\to$ pair-of-pants/genus surface (1-handle, saddle) $\to$ closed torus (2-handle, max). The dot in the height bar marks $c$; coloured handles are the new piece glued at each crossing.

See also
  • Smooth manifolds · gradient flow integrates a vector field; we use it to push $M^b$ onto $M^a$.

Used in
Morse theory · CW structure from a Morse function
</section>

3. CW structure

A $k$-handle $D^k\times D^{n-k}$ deformation-retracts onto its core disk $D^k\times\{0\}$. Iterating Theorem B and contracting each handle to its core, the manifold $M$ has the homotopy type of a CW complex with one cell of dimension $k$ for every critical point of $f$ of Morse index $k$.

CW theorem (Milnor). Every closed smooth manifold $M$ admitting a Morse function $f$ is homotopy equivalent to a CW complex with one $k$-cell for each index-$k$ critical point of $f$.

This is the bridge from differential to algebraic topology: smooth analysis (Morse theory) computes the same invariants as cellular methods (CW homology). Concrete consequence — the Euler characteristic $$\chi(M) \;=\; \sum_k (-1)^k c_k(f)$$ where $c_k(f)$ counts the index-$k$ critical points. Independent of the Morse function chosen.

Widget · matching critical points to cellsclick a critical point on the torus to highlight its cell
click a critical point

Left: torus with the four critical points of the standard height function (one min, two saddles, one max). Right: corresponding CW structure on $T^2$ with one 0-cell, two 1-cells, and one 2-cell. Clicking matches the index-$k$ critical point to its $k$-cell.

See also
  • Singular homology

Used in
Morse theory · Morse homology
Morse theory · Morse inequalities
</section>

4. Morse inequalities

Let $b_k(M)=\mathrm{rank}\,H_k(M;\mathbb{Q})$ be the $k$-th Betti number. Since $b_k(M)$ is bounded above by the rank of $C_k$ in any cellular chain complex, we get the weak Morse inequalities: $$b_k(M) \;\le\; c_k(f) \quad \text{for every } k.$$ The strong Morse inequalities sharpen this: $$\sum_{k\le j} (-1)^{j-k}\bigl(c_k(f) - b_k(M)\bigr) \;\ge\; 0 \quad \text{for every } j,$$ with equality at $j=n$ (Morse–Reeb / Lefschetz: the alternating sum is $\chi(M)$).

Widget · Betti vs Morse counts on $\Sigma_g$drag $g$; see how counts grow
$g=2$

Bar chart per index: $b_0=1$, $b_1=2g$, $b_2=1$ (Betti numbers of $\Sigma_g$), and minimal $c_k = b_k$ for the standard height function on the obvious embedding. Equality everywhere — the standard height function is perfect. The Euler characteristic $\chi(\Sigma_g)=2-2g$ falls out as $1 - 2g + 1$.

A Morse function with $c_k(f)=b_k(M)$ for all $k$ is called perfect. Perfect Morse functions exist on $\mathbb{C}P^n$, on flag varieties, and on compact Lie groups (Bott). They do not exist on every manifold: the strong inequalities can be strict.

See also
  • Algebraic topology · singular homology and Betti numbers.

Used in
Morse theory · Applications and generalisations
</section>

5. Morse homology

The Morse inequalities motivate a chain complex whose generators are the critical points. Pick a Riemannian metric $g$ on $M$ so that $-\nabla f$ has integral curves, and assume $(f,g)$ is a Morse–Smale pair: stable and unstable manifolds of critical points intersect transversally.

Define $$C_k(f) \;=\; \mathbb{Z}\langle\,p \in \mathrm{Crit}(f) : \ind(p)=k\,\rangle.$$ For $\ind(p)=k$ and $\ind(q)=k-1$ the moduli space of unparameterised gradient trajectories from $p$ to $q$ is a finite set (under Morse–Smale + compactness); count signed: $$\partial p \;=\; \sum_{\ind(q)=k-1} \#\mathcal{M}(p,q) \cdot q.$$

Theorem. $\partial^2 = 0$, and the resulting homology $HM_*(M;f,g)$ is independent of the Morse–Smale pair; there is a canonical isomorphism $$HM_*(M) \;\cong\; H_*(M;\mathbb{Z}).$$

Widget · gradient flow lines on the torusclick a saddle to see its descending and ascending trajectories
click a saddle

For the upright torus $T^2$ with $f=z$: from each saddle, two trajectories descend to the minimum (canceling in pairs over $\mathbb{Z}/2$) and two ascend to the maximum. The Morse complex is $\mathbb{Z}^1 \xleftarrow{0} \mathbb{Z}^2 \xleftarrow{0} \mathbb{Z}^1$; Morse homology equals singular homology of $T^2$: $H_0=\mathbb{Z}, H_1=\mathbb{Z}^2, H_2=\mathbb{Z}$.

See also
  • Differential forms · the dual story is de Rham cohomology; perfect Morse functions match Hodge theory.

Used in
Morse theory · Applications and generalisations
</section>

6. Applications and generalisations

Even before Morse homology, the theory has bite.

Reeb's sphere theorem

If a closed connected $M^n$ admits a Morse function with exactly two critical points, then $M$ is homeomorphic to $S^n$. (The two critical points must be a min and a max — index $0$ and $n$ — so $M$ is two disks glued along their common boundary.) This was Milnor's route to the exotic 7-spheres: certain $S^3$-bundles over $S^4$ admit such a "two-critical-point" $f$ but are not diffeomorphic to the standard $S^7$.

$S^2$ vs $\mathbb{R}P^2$

A direct contrast. The standard height function on $S^2$ has 2 critical points: indices $0,2$, so $\chi(S^2)=1-0+1=2$. The minimal Morse function on $\mathbb{R}P^2$ has 3 critical points: indices $0,1,2$, giving $\chi(\mathbb{R}P^2)=1-1+1=1$. The extra index-$1$ critical point is not optional: $\mathbb{R}P^2$ is non-orientable, $b_1(\mathbb{R}P^2;\mathbb{Q})=0$ but the $\mathbb{Z}/2$ Betti number is $1$, and Morse counts respect that.

Widget · $S^2$ vs $\mathbb{RP}^2$ via Morse countstoggle

$S^2$ as a CW complex: one 0-cell + one 2-cell, $\chi=2$. $\mathbb{RP}^2$ as a CW complex: one 0-cell + one 1-cell + one 2-cell, $\chi=1$. The 1-cell is the Möbius core that distinguishes $\mathbb{RP}^2$ from the sphere.

Bott and infinite dimensions

Bott observed that Morse theory works nearly as well when critical points are replaced by non-degenerate critical submanifolds (Morse–Bott theory). Applied to the energy functional on path/loop spaces of compact Lie groups, this gave Bott periodicity — one of the cleanest periodicity statements in topology.

The further leap: in the 1980s, Floer realised the action functional on the loop space of a symplectic manifold is "Morse" in a formal sense. Critical points are periodic orbits of a Hamiltonian; gradient flow lines are pseudo-holomorphic cylinders; the analogue of Morse homology is Floer homology, which proved the Arnold conjecture and is now ubiquitous in symplectic topology.
</section>

7. Connections

Morse theory is a hinge between three different ways of looking at a smooth manifold — analysis (gradients, flow), topology (cells, homology), and combinatorics (the index data). Each thread points to a sibling page that picks up where this one stops.

Differential geometry and gradient flow

The negative gradient flow $\dot \gamma = -\nabla f$ is the workhorse of differential geometry applications: heat-flow regularisation, Łojasiewicz inequalities, mean-curvature-flow analysis. The Morse stratification is the prototype every gradient-flow argument generalises.

Algebraic topology and CW homology

Singular homology, the Mayer–Vietoris sequence, and CW-complex homology are the algebraic targets — Morse homology is isomorphic to all three for compact $M$. The Morse inequalities are a numerical witness that this isomorphism is non-trivial: $f$ has at least the topology of $M$ baked in.

Lie groups and homogeneous spaces

Bott’s theorem on the homotopy of compact Lie groups uses Morse theory of the energy functional on the loop space of $G$. Equivariant Morse theory (Atiyah–Bott) extends to manifolds with group actions and underlies the Witten-style proofs of Atiyah–Singer.

Symplectic and Floer-theoretic generalisations

Floer’s insight: do Morse theory on the symplectic action functional on the loop space of a symplectic manifold. Characteristic classes and pseudo-holomorphic curves take the place of the Hessian; Floer homology generalises Morse homology to infinite-dimensional settings (Lagrangian, Hamiltonian, instanton, monopole, Heegaard).

Open frontiers: discrete Morse theory and topological data analysis (Forman, persistent homology); $A_\infty$- and $L_\infty$-Morse theory (Fukaya, Kontsevich); equivariant and twisted Floer theories (Seiberg–Witten, Heegaard, embedded contact); the symplectic-cobordism / Khovanov-homology bridge.

<script>
/* § 1 height function on torus */
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById("m-svg");
  const tilt = document.getElementById("m-tilt");
  const tiltOut = document.getElementById("m-tilt-out");
  const out = document.getElementById("m-out");
  if(!svg) return;
  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "<title>Widget · height function on the torus</title>";
    const cx=380, cy=160;
    const R=110, r=40;
    const theta = parseFloat(tilt.value) * Math.PI/180;
    // outer ellipse
    const aO=R+r, bO=(R+r)*Math.cos(theta);
    const aI=R-r, bI=(R-r)*Math.cos(theta);
    svg.appendChild(SVG("ellipse",{cx,cy,rx:aO,ry:bO,fill:"var(--panel2)",stroke:"var(--blue)","stroke-width":1.5}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("ellipse",{cx,cy,rx:aI,ry:bI,fill:"var(--panel)",stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1,"stroke-dasharray":"4 3"}));
    // critical points: top max, bottom min, two saddles on inner equator
    const pts = [
      {x:cx, y:cy-bO, k:2, name:"max"},
      {x:cx, y:cy+bO, k:0, name:"min"},
      {x:cx-aI, y:cy, k:1, name:"saddle"},
      {x:cx+aI, y:cy, k:1, name:"saddle"}
    ];
    const colorFor = k => k===0?"var(--green)":(k===2?"var(--pink)":"var(--yellow)");
    for(const p of pts){
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:p.x,cy:p.y,r:7,fill:colorFor(p.k),stroke:"#111","stroke-width":1.5}));
      const t = SVG("text",{x:p.x+12,y:p.y+4,"font-size":12,fill:colorFor(p.k)});
      t.textContent = `index ${p.k}`;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // height bar
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:90,y1:50,x2:90,y2:270,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:90,y:42,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "z";
    // legend
    const lg = SVG("g");
    const items = [["index 0 (min)","var(--green)"],["index 1 (saddle)","var(--yellow)"],["index 2 (max)","var(--pink)"]];
    items.forEach((it,i)=>{
      lg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:600,cy:60+i*22,r:6,fill:it[1]}));
      const t = SVG("text",{x:614,y:64+i*22,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--ink)"});
      t.textContent = it[0];
      lg.appendChild(t);
    });
    svg.appendChild(lg);
    tiltOut.textContent = `${tilt.value}°`;
    out.textContent = `c_0 = 1, c_1 = 2, c_2 = 1   →   χ(T²) = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0   ✓`;
  }
  tilt.addEventListener("input", draw);
  draw();
})();
</script>

<script>
/* § 2 sublevel sets */
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById("h-svg");
  const c = document.getElementById("h-c");
  const cOut = document.getElementById("h-c-out");
  const out = document.getElementById("h-out");
  const playBtn = document.getElementById("h-play");
  if(!svg) return;
  let timer = null;
  // critical values normalised: 0 (min), 35 (saddle 1), 65 (saddle 2), 100 (max)
  const crits = [
    {v:0, k:0, name:"minimum"},
    {v:35, k:1, name:"saddle 1"},
    {v:65, k:1, name:"saddle 2"},
    {v:100, k:2, name:"maximum"}
  ];
  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "<title>Widget · sublevel sets of a torus height function</title>";
    const cval = parseFloat(c.value);
    cOut.textContent = `c = ${(cval/10).toFixed(1)}`;
    // height bar on left
    const x0=70, y0=40, y1=300;
    const yFor = v => y1 - (v/100)*(y1-y0);
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0,y1:y0,x2:x0,y2:y1,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":2}));
    // critical-value tick marks
    for(const cr of crits){
      const y = yFor(cr.v);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0-6,y1:y,x2:x0+6,y2:y,stroke:cr.k===0?"var(--green)":(cr.k===2?"var(--pink)":"var(--yellow)"),"stroke-width":2}));
      const t = SVG("text",{x:x0-12,y:y+4,"text-anchor":"end","font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t.textContent = cr.name;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // current c marker
    const cy = yFor(Math.max(0, Math.min(100, cval)));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:x0,cy:cy,r:6,fill:"var(--cyan)",stroke:"#111","stroke-width":1.5}));
    // axis label
    const ax = SVG("text",{x:x0,y:y0-12,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    ax.textContent = "f";
    svg.appendChild(ax);
    // sublevel-set diagram on right
    const dx = 280, dy = 170, dw = 380;
    // count handles attached so far
    let handlesAttached = crits.filter(cr => cval >= cr.v).length;
    let label = "";
    if(cval < 0){ label = "M^c = ∅ (below min)"; }
    else if(handlesAttached === 1){ label = "M^c ≃ disk D² (after 0-handle)"; }
    else if(handlesAttached === 2){ label = "M^c ≃ cylinder D¹×S¹ (1-handle attached)"; }
    else if(handlesAttached === 3){ label = "M^c ≃ pair-of-pants core (2nd 1-handle attached)"; }
    else if(handlesAttached === 4){ label = "M^c = T² (after 2-handle)"; }
    // schematic shapes
    const colorByH = ["#0000","var(--green)","var(--yellow)","var(--yellow)","var(--pink)"];
    if(handlesAttached >= 1){
      // 0-handle: disk
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:dx,cy:dy,r:34,fill:"var(--panel2)",stroke:"var(--green)","stroke-width":2}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:dx,y:dy+5,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "D²";
    }
    if(handlesAttached >= 2){
      // first saddle: 1-handle band stretching to right
      svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d:`M ${dx+34} ${dy-22} q 50 -8 100 0 q 50 8 100 -2 q -10 12 0 28 q -50 14 -100 0 q -50 -14 -100 0 z`,
        fill:"var(--panel)",stroke:"var(--yellow)","stroke-width":2}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:dx+150,y:dy+6,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--yellow)"})).textContent = "1-handle";
    }
    if(handlesAttached >= 3){
      // second saddle: pair-of-pants splitting
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:dx+260,cy:dy-22,r:18,fill:"none",stroke:"var(--yellow)","stroke-width":2}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:dx+260,cy:dy+22,r:18,fill:"none",stroke:"var(--yellow)","stroke-width":2}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:dx+330,y:dy,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--yellow)"})).textContent = "+1-handle";
    }
    if(handlesAttached >= 4){
      // 2-handle: cap onto the "inside" — show as filled torus icon
      svg.appendChild(SVG("ellipse",{cx:dx+170,cy:dy+90,rx:130,ry:30,fill:"var(--panel2)",stroke:"var(--pink)","stroke-width":2}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("ellipse",{cx:dx+170,cy:dy+90,rx:60,ry:14,fill:"var(--panel)",stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1,"stroke-dasharray":"3 3"}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:dx+170,y:dy+135,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--pink)"})).textContent = "2-handle → T²";
    }
    if(handlesAttached === 0){
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:dx+150,y:dy,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "M^c = ∅";
    }
    out.textContent = `${label}     handles attached: ${handlesAttached} of 4`;
  }
  c.addEventListener("input", draw);
  playBtn.addEventListener("click", () => {
    if(timer){ clearInterval(timer); timer=null; playBtn.textContent="play"; return; }
    playBtn.textContent = "pause";
    if(parseFloat(c.value) >= 110) c.value = -10;
    timer = setInterval(()=>{
      let v = parseFloat(c.value) + 1;
      if(v > 110){ clearInterval(timer); timer=null; playBtn.textContent="play"; return; }
      c.value = v; draw();
    }, 60);
  });
  draw();
})();
</script>

    }
    // labels
    svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:160,y:30,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "T² with critical points";
    svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:sx+sw/2,y:60,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "CW model: aba⁻¹b⁻¹";
  }
  highlight(-1);
  svg.addEventListener("click", (ev)=>{
    const t = ev.target;
    if(t && t.dataset && t.dataset.idx !== undefined){
      const i = parseInt(t.dataset.idx,10);
      const p = cps[i];
      out.textContent = `${p.label} (Morse index ${p.k}) ↔ ${p.k}-cell highlighted on the right`;
      highlight(i);
    }
  });
})();
</script>

<script>
/* § 4 Morse inequalities bar chart */
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById("ineq-svg");
  const g = document.getElementById("ineq-g");
  const gOut = document.getElementById("ineq-g-out");
  const out = document.getElementById("ineq-out");
  if(!svg) return;
  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "<title>Widget · Betti numbers vs Morse counts on a genus-g surface</title>";
    const G = parseInt(g.value, 10);
    gOut.textContent = `g=${G}`;
    const b = [1, 2*G, 1];
    const c = [1, 2*G, 1];
    const chi = b[0] - b[1] + b[2];
    // chart
    const x0=60, y0=40, w=620, h=200, by=y0+h;
    const maxV = Math.max(2, b[1], 4);
    const barW = 60;
    const groupGap = 30;
    const groupW = barW*2 + 12;
    const startX = 130;
    for(let k=0;k<3;k++){
      const cx = startX + k*(groupW + groupGap);
      // Betti
      const bH = (b[k]/maxV)*h*0.8;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:cx,y:by-bH,width:barW,height:bH,fill:"var(--cyan)",stroke:"#111"}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx+barW/2,y:by-bH-6,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--cyan)"})).textContent = `b${k}=${b[k]}`;
      // Morse
      const cH = (c[k]/maxV)*h*0.8;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:cx+barW+12,y:by-cH,width:barW,height:cH,fill:"var(--yellow)",stroke:"#111"}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx+barW+12+barW/2,y:by-cH-6,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--yellow)"})).textContent = `c${k}=${c[k]}`;
      // index label
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx+groupW/2,y:by+18,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--ink)"})).textContent = `index ${k}`;
    }
    // axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0,y1:by,x2:x0+w,y2:by,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1}));
    // legend
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:580,y:50,width:14,height:14,fill:"var(--cyan)"}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:600,y:62,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--cyan)"})).textContent = "b_k (Betti)";
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:580,y:72,width:14,height:14,fill:"var(--yellow)"}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:600,y:84,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--yellow)"})).textContent = "c_k (Morse)";
    out.textContent = `Σ_g, g=${G}:  b = (${b.join(", ")}),  c = (${c.join(", ")}),  χ = ${chi}.   Equality b_k = c_k everywhere → standard height function is perfect.`;
  }
  g.addEventListener("input", draw);
  draw();
})();
</script>

<script>
/* § 5 gradient flow on torus */
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById("fl-svg");
  const out = document.getElementById("fl-out");
  if(!svg) return;
  const cx=380, cy=160, R=130, r=46;
  const max = {x:cx, y:cy-r-50, label:"max", color:"var(--pink)"};
  const min = {x:cx, y:cy+r+50, label:"min", color:"var(--green)"};
  const sad1 = {x:cx-R, y:cy, label:"saddle a", color:"var(--yellow)"};
  const sad2 = {x:cx+R, y:cy, label:"saddle b", color:"var(--yellow)"};
  function draw(highlight){
    svg.innerHTML = "<title>Widget · gradient flow on the torus</title>";
    // outer torus
    svg.appendChild(SVG("ellipse",{cx,cy,rx:R+r,ry:r+10,fill:"var(--panel2)",stroke:"var(--blue)","stroke-width":1.5}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("ellipse",{cx,cy,rx:R-r,ry:r-26,fill:"var(--panel)",stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1,"stroke-dasharray":"3 3"}));
    // draw flow lines from selected saddle (or all)
    function flowsFromSaddle(s){
      // descending: two trajectories to min
      drawArrow(svg,[s.x,s.y],[min.x-30,min.y],{color:"var(--green)",curve:30,markerId:"arr-down1",width:1.5,label:"−∇f"});
      drawArrow(svg,[s.x,s.y],[min.x+30,min.y],{color:"var(--green)",curve:-30,markerId:"arr-down2",width:1.5});
      // ascending: two trajectories to max
      drawArrow(svg,[s.x,s.y],[max.x-30,max.y],{color:"var(--pink)",curve:-30,markerId:"arr-up1",width:1.5,label:"+∇f"});
      drawArrow(svg,[s.x,s.y],[max.x+30,max.y],{color:"var(--pink)",curve:30,markerId:"arr-up2",width:1.5});
    }
    if(highlight==="a") flowsFromSaddle(sad1);
    else if(highlight==="b") flowsFromSaddle(sad2);
    // critical points
    [max,min,sad1,sad2].forEach(p=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:p.x,cy:p.y,r:8,fill:p.color,stroke:"#111","stroke-width":1.5,style:"cursor:pointer","data-label":p.label}));
      const t = SVG("text",{x:p.x,y:p.y-14,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:p.color,style:"pointer-events:none"});
      t.textContent = p.label;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    // chain complex display
    const cy2 = 290;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx,y:cy2,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "Morse complex:  ℤ¹  ←0—  ℤ² (saddles)  ←0—  ℤ¹     →  HM_*(T²) = ℤ, ℤ², ℤ";
  }
  svg.addEventListener("click", ev => {
    const t = ev.target;
    if(t && t.dataset && t.dataset.label){
      const lbl = t.dataset.label;
      if(lbl === "saddle a"){ draw("a"); out.textContent = "Saddle a: 2 descending trajectories → minimum, 2 ascending → maximum. Boundary count over ℤ/2 is 0."; }
      else if(lbl === "saddle b"){ draw("b"); out.textContent = "Saddle b: 2 descending trajectories → minimum, 2 ascending → maximum. Same picture, independent generator of H_1."; }
      else { draw(null); out.textContent = `${lbl}: click a saddle to see its trajectories.`; }
    }
  });
  draw(null);
})();
</script>

<script>
/* § 6 S^2 vs RP^2 */
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById("app-svg");
  const out = document.getElementById("app-out");
  const bS = document.getElementById("app-s2");
  const bR = document.getElementById("app-rp2");
  if(!svg) return;
  function draw(mode){
    svg.innerHTML = "<title>Widget · S² vs RP² Morse decompositions</title>";
    // left: critical-point list
    const lx=120, ly=60;
    let pts;
    if(mode==="s2"){
      pts = [
        {label:"min",  k:0, color:"var(--green)"},
        {label:"max",  k:2, color:"var(--pink)"}
      ];
    } else {
      pts = [
        {label:"min",    k:0, color:"var(--green)"},
        {label:"saddle", k:1, color:"var(--yellow)"},
        {label:"max",    k:2, color:"var(--pink)"}
      ];
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:lx,y:30,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "critical points";
    pts.forEach((p,i)=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:lx,cy:ly+i*48,r:9,fill:p.color,stroke:"#111","stroke-width":1.5}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:lx+22,y:ly+i*48+5,"font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)"})).textContent = `${p.label} (index ${p.k})`;
    });
    // right: CW cells stacked
    const cx=440, cy=140;
    if(mode==="s2"){
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx+90,y:30,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "CW model of S²";
      // 0-cell
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx,cy:cy,r:80,fill:"rgba(224,122,95,0.18)",stroke:"var(--pink)","stroke-width":2}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx,y:cy+5,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--pink)"})).textContent = "2-cell";
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx,cy:cy-80,r:7,fill:"var(--green)",stroke:"#111"}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx,y:cy-95,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--green)"})).textContent = "0-cell";
    } else {
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx+90,y:30,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"})).textContent = "CW model of RP²";
      // 2-cell disk
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx,cy:cy,r:80,fill:"rgba(224,122,95,0.18)",stroke:"var(--pink)","stroke-width":2}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx,y:cy+5,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--pink)"})).textContent = "2-cell";
      // 1-cell as a loop (with antipodal identification)
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx,cy:cy,r:80,fill:"none",stroke:"var(--yellow)","stroke-width":3}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx+88,y:cy+5,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--yellow)"})).textContent = "1-cell (Möbius core)";
      // antipodal arrows
      svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d:`M ${cx-80} ${cy} A 80 80 0 0 1 ${cx+80} ${cy}`,fill:"none",stroke:"var(--yellow)","stroke-width":2,"stroke-dasharray":"5 4"}));
      // 0-cell
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx-80,cy:cy,r:7,fill:"var(--green)",stroke:"#111"}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx+80,cy:cy,r:7,fill:"var(--green)",stroke:"#111"}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx-80,y:cy+22,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--green)"})).textContent = "0-cell";
      svg.appendChild(SVG("text",{x:cx+80,y:cy+22,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--green)"})).textContent = "= 0-cell";
    }
    if(mode==="s2"){
      out.textContent = "S²: c = (1, 0, 1).   χ = 1 - 0 + 1 = 2.   Two critical points — Reeb's theorem applies.";
    } else {
      out.textContent = "RP²: c = (1, 1, 1).   χ = 1 - 1 + 1 = 1.   The index-1 critical point realises the non-orientable Möbius core.";
    }
    bS.classList.toggle("active", mode==="s2");
    bR.classList.toggle("active", mode==="rp2");
  }
  bS.addEventListener("click", () => draw("s2"));
  bR.addEventListener("click", () => draw("rp2"));
  draw("s2");
})();
</script>

<script>
/* sidebar TOC builder + active-section highlighting */
(function(){
  function build(){
    const aside = document.querySelector("aside.sidetoc");
    const topnav = document.querySelector("nav.toc");
    if(!aside || !topnav) return;
    const srcs = [...topnav.querySelectorAll('a[href^="#"]')];
    if(!srcs.length) return;
    const title = document.createElement("div");
    title.className = "sidetoc-title";
    title.textContent = "On this page";
    aside.appendChild(title);
    const ol = document.createElement("ol");
    srcs.forEach(src => {
      const li = document.createElement("li");
      const a = document.createElement("a");
      a.href = src.getAttribute("href");
      a.textContent = src.textContent.replace(/ /g," ").trim();
      li.appendChild(a); ol.appendChild(li);
    });
    aside.appendChild(ol);
    const links = [...aside.querySelectorAll("a")];
    const sections = [];
    links.forEach(a => {
      const id = a.getAttribute("href").slice(1);
      const s = document.getElementById(id);
      if(s) sections.push({a, s});
    });
    if(!sections.length) return;
    function update(){
      const threshold = 140;
      let activeLink = sections[0].a;
      for(const {a,s} of sections){
        if(s.getBoundingClientRect().top <= threshold) activeLink = a;
      }
      links.forEach(x => x.classList.remove("active"));
      activeLink.classList.add("active");
    }
    update();
    window.addEventListener("scroll", update, {passive:true});
    window.addEventListener("resize", update);
  }
  if(document.readyState === "loading") document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", build);
  else build();
})();
</script>

})();
